summary: The synthetic context-pack packet is a valid AIQL review surface only when the empty audit fields remain an explicit opaque-evidence boundary rather than implied proof that real source checks already passed.

findings.title: Empty audit fields must remain an explicit synthetic boundary
findings.summary: The omitted research-source audit and public-source list are acceptable only because the packet explicitly uses opaque evidence labels and synthetic scope; future edits must not imply hidden source checks.

findings.title: Automated downstream readiness is unsupported
findings.summary: The packet does not supply source freshness, approval, routing, or public-source selection evidence, so it cannot support automated downstream action.
evidence: Known gaps: source freshness, owner approval, action priority

findings.title: Opaque evidence labels limit standalone traceability
findings.summary: Generic evidence labels preserve the open-source boundary but require caller-owned registry resolution before a downstream reviewer can treat them as actionable support.

findings.title: The shared packet must not become a memory store or tracker mirror
findings.summary: The public-safe seam works because AIQL reviews pack quality only; retrieval, interpretation, retention, and routing stay caller-owned.
evidence: Boundary note: caller-owned retrieval and routing

follow_up: Keep the empty research-source audit and public-source list documented as intentional synthetic scope whenever this example is copied.
Require embedding repos to resolve opaque evidence labels through their own registry before using the packet for any real decision or routing step.
Avoid extracting source-audit or context-pack automation until repeated public use cases justify a narrower generic seam.

provenance.label: Privacy boundary
provenance.value: No public-source list or copied source truth in shared artifact

markdown:
# Synthetic Context Pack Quality Review

Output classification: review

## Summary

The synthetic ContextWeave-style pack-quality packet is a valid AIQL review surface because it stays bounded to one downstream review, uses opaque evidence labels instead of copied source truth, and explicitly leaves the research-source audit and public-source list empty. That omission must remain a visible synthetic boundary rather than being mistaken for proof that any real web, registry, tracker, or approval check already passed.
